content.body:
Snell's Law: n1 sin(theta1) = n2 sin(theta2)
n = c/v (refractive index)

Total Internal Reflection (TIR):
Occurs when: light in denser medium, angle > critical angle
sin(critical angle) = 1/n (going to air)

Optical fibre: TIR keeps light bouncing along fibre
Applications: internet data, medical endoscopes

content.title: Snell's Law Examples
content.body:
Air (n=1) to glass (n=1.5), theta1=60 degrees
1 x sin60 = 1.5 x sin(theta2)
sin(theta2) = 0.866/1.5 = 0.577
theta2 = 35.3 degrees (bends toward normal)

Critical angle for glass (n=1.5):
sin(c) = 1/1.5 = 0.667, c = 41.8 degrees
Any incidence angle > 41.8: TIR

content.title: Applications
content.body:
Optical fibres: internet data, endoscopes (TIR)
Diamond: n=2.42, critical angle=24.4 degrees, brilliant sparkle
Mirage: hot air near ground has lower n, light curves upward
Periscope: glass prisms use TIR at 45 degrees
Fish-eye view from underwater: compressed to 97 degree cone above (critical angle 49 deg for water)

questions.question: Snell's law: n1 sin1 = n2 sin2. Light glass (n=1.5) to air, theta=30. sin(air angle)=
questions.explanation: 1.5 x sin30 = 1 x sin(air): sin = 1.5x0.5 = 0.75, theta = 48.6 degrees

questions.question: Total internal reflection requires
options: light going denser to less dense AND angle greater than critical angle
questions.answer: light going denser to less dense AND angle greater than critical angle
questions.explanation: TIR conditions: (1) denser medium to less dense, (2) angle of incidence exceeds critical angle

questions.question: Critical angle for diamond n=2.42. sin(c) = 1/2.42 = 0.413. Critical angle =
questions.explanation: sin-1(0.413) = 24.4 degrees. Very small means most light internally reflects - gives brilliant sparkle

questions.question: Optical fibres transmit data using
options: light pulses by total internal reflection along glass fibre
questions.answer: light pulses by total internal reflection along glass fibre
questions.explanation: Light hits glass-cladding interface at angle exceeding critical angle - TIR. Light travels with minimal loss over km

questions.question: Refractive index n = c/v. If v = 2x10^8 m/s in glass, n =
Light bends TOWARD normal when entering denser medium because
options: it slows down in denser medium - wavefronts crowd together - direction changes toward normal
questions.answer: it slows down in denser medium - wavefronts crowd together - direction changes toward normal
questions.explanation: Huygens principle: slower speed in dense medium causes bending. Like wheels on rough ground - one side slows first

questions.question: Dispersion by prism occurs because
options: different wavelengths have different refractive indices: violet bends most, red bends least
questions.answer: different wavelengths have different refractive indices: violet bends most, red bends least
questions.explanation: n varies with wavelength - shorter wavelength (violet) has higher n. ROYGBIV spectrum emerges

questions.question: Swimming pool looks shallower than it is because
options: refraction at water-air surface bends rays away from normal - apparent depth less than real depth
questions.answer: refraction at water-air surface bends rays away from normal - apparent depth less than real depth
questions.explanation: Light from pool bottom bends away from normal at surface. Eye traces straight line back - sees image closer than actual floor

questions.question: Endoscope uses optical fibre bundles to
options: carry illumination in and image out of body cavities using TIR - minimally invasive surgery
questions.answer: carry illumination in and image out of body cavities using TIR - minimally invasive surgery
questions.explanation: Coherent fibre bundle: preserves spatial arrangement - transmits image faithfully. Surgeon sees internal organs without major surgery

questions.question: Mirage in desert: hot road appears wet because
options: hot air near ground has lower density and n - light curves upward forming reflected image of sky
questions.answer: hot air near ground has lower density and n - light curves upward forming reflected image of sky
questions.explanation: Temperature gradient creates n gradient. Light bends like TIR - sky light curved up to eye appears as bright patch on road

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
Lens formula: 1/f = 1/v + 1/u
f = focal length, v = image distance, u = object distance
(Real positive convention: real object u positive, real image v positive)

Magnification m = v/u = image height / object height
Power P = 1/f (Diopters, D)

Converging (convex): f positive, can form real or virtual image
Diverging (concave): f negative, always virtual upright image

content.title: Ray Diagrams - Converging Lens
content.body:
Object beyond 2f: real, inverted, diminished (camera)
Object at 2f: real, inverted, same size (photocopier)
Object between f and 2f: real, inverted, magnified (projector)
Object inside f: virtual, upright, magnified (magnifying glass)

Diverging lens: always virtual, upright, diminished image

content.title: Eye Defects
content.body:
Normal vision: image formed exactly on retina
Myopia (short-sighted): eyeball too long, image in front of retina - correct with concave (diverging) lens
Hypermetropia (long-sighted): eyeball too short, image behind retina - correct with convex (converging) lens
Presbyopia: lens stiffens with age, cannot accommodate - reading glasses needed

questions.question: Lens formula f=20cm, u=30cm. Find v.
Magnification m = v/u. v=60cm, u=30cm. m=
questions.explanation: m = 60/30 = 2 (image twice as large, real and inverted)

questions.question: Lens power P = 1/f (Diopters). f = 0.25m. P =
questions.explanation: P = 1/0.25 = 4 Diopters

questions.question: Magnifying glass works with object
questions.explanation: Object inside f: diverging rays - appears to come from larger virtual image. Brain perceives enlarged upright image

questions.question: Myopia corrected by
questions.explanation: Myopia: eyeball too long, focal point in front of retina. Concave lens diverges rays before entering eye - moves focal point to retina

questions.question: Camera forms image
questions.explanation: Distant object (u >> 2f): real, inverted, diminished image on film/sensor. Shutter controls exposure time

questions.question: Increasing lens power means
options: shorter focal length - more curved lens
questions.answer: shorter focal length - more curved lens
questions.explanation: P = 1/f: higher power = shorter focal length. Thicker, more curved lens bends light more strongly

questions.question: Compound microscope magnification = objective magnification times eyepiece magnification. If obj=40x, eye=10x, total=
questions.explanation: Total magnification = 40 x 10 = 400x. Objective creates real intermediate image; eyepiece magnifies that

questions.question: Diverging lens always produces image that is
questions.explanation: Concave/diverging lens: regardless of object position, always produces virtual, upright, smaller image. Cannot project on screen

questions.question: Long-sightedness (hypermetropia) corrected by
questions.explanation: Hypermetropia: eyeball too short, image forms behind retina. Converging lens adds refractive power - brings focus forward onto retina

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
Electromagnetic spectrum (decreasing wavelength):
Radio > Microwave > IR > Visible (700-400nm) > UV > X-ray > Gamma

Young's double slit:
y = lambda x L / d
y=fringe spacing, lambda=wavelength, L=screen distance, d=slit separation

Diffraction grating: d sin(theta) = n lambda
d=grating spacing, n=order number

content.title: Young's Double Slit
content.body:
lambda=600nm, d=0.5mm, L=2m
y = 600e-9 x 2 / 0.5e-3 = 2.4mm

Increasing lambda: wider fringes
Increasing d: narrower fringes
Increasing L: wider fringes

Interference proves light is a WAVE:
Constructive: path difference = n lambda (bright)
Destructive: path difference = (n+0.5) lambda (dark)

content.title: EM Spectrum Applications
content.body:
Radio: broadcasting, GPS, mobile phones
Microwave: cooking (water resonance), WiFi, radar
IR: heat detection, remote controls, night vision
Visible: vision, photography
UV: sterilisation, fluorescence, vitamin D production
X-ray: medical imaging, airport security
Gamma: cancer treatment, sterilising medical equipment, nuclear

questions.question: Fringe spacing increases when
questions.explanation: y is proportional to lambda. Red light (700nm) gives wider fringes than blue light (400nm)

questions.question: Double slit: lambda=500nm, d=0.2mm, L=1m. y=
Young's double slit demonstrates light is
options: a transverse wave (interference pattern proves wave nature)
questions.answer: a transverse wave (interference pattern proves wave nature)
questions.explanation: Interference requires waves. Alternating bright and dark fringes from constructive and destructive interference

questions.question: Diffraction grating: d=2 micrometres, lambda=500nm, n=1. sin(theta)=
questions.explanation: sin theta = n lambda/d = 1 x 500e-9 / 2e-6 = 0.25, theta = 14.5 degrees

questions.question: All EM waves in vacuum travel at
questions.explanation: All EM waves: same speed c = 3x10^8 m/s in vacuum. They differ only in wavelength (and hence frequency and energy)

questions.question: Microwaves heat food by
options: causing water molecules to rotate/vibrate - friction generates heat
questions.answer: causing water molecules to rotate/vibrate - friction generates heat
questions.explanation: 2.45GHz microwaves match rotational frequency of water molecules. Energy absorbed - water heats up - cooks food

questions.question: UV causes sunburn because
options: UV photons have enough energy (E=hf) to break chemical bonds in DNA - causing mutations
questions.answer: UV photons have enough energy (E=hf) to break chemical bonds in DNA - causing mutations
questions.explanation: UV-B and UV-C break DNA bonds in skin cells. Can cause mutations leading to skin cancer. Ozone layer absorbs UV-C

questions.question: Polarisation proves light is
options: a transverse wave with oscillations perpendicular to propagation
questions.answer: a transverse wave with oscillations perpendicular to propagation
questions.explanation: Only transverse waves can be polarised. Polaroid filter absorbs one orientation of oscillation. Sound (longitudinal) cannot be polarised

questions.question: X-rays used in medicine because
options: they penetrate soft tissue but are absorbed by dense bone - differential absorption creates shadow image
questions.answer: they penetrate soft tissue but are absorbed by dense bone - differential absorption creates shadow image
questions.explanation: X-rays: high energy, short wavelength. Penetrate soft tissue, absorbed by calcium in bone. White areas on X-ray = bone

questions.question: Coherent light needed for stable interference. Coherent means
options: same frequency and constant phase difference
questions.answer: same frequency and constant constant phase difference
questions.explanation: Coherence: same frequency and stable phase relationship. Laser light is coherent - gives stable interference. Ordinary bulb light is incoherent

questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Optics Full concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Optics Full builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Optics Full. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Optics Full. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Optics Full include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Optics Full. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
